name: Numelvi
description: Second-generation selective JAK1 inhibitor (10x+ selectivity over JAK2/JAK3/TYK2) for allergic dermatitis. No vaccine interaction warnings. Dogs 6+ months. Give with food. FDA approved Feb 2026.
common_side_effects: Vomiting/nausea (6.9% vs 4.2% placebo), Otitis externa (6.3%), Hematuria (4.9%), Anorexia (4.2%), Diarrhea (4.2% vs 10.4% placebo), Crystalluria (3.5%), Lethargy (3.5%), Upset stomach/flatulence/bloating (2.1%). Lab: leukopenia, elevated ALT/AST. Safety study: demodicosis at 5X dose
side_effects_sources: [FDA NADA 141-596 FOI](https://animaldrugsatfda.fda.gov/adafda/app/search/public/document/downloadFoi/18155) (n=144 treated vs n=144 placebo, 28 days)

name: Zenrelia
description: Non-selective JAK inhibitor (JAK1/JAK2/TYK2) for canine atopic dermatitis. Immunosuppressive. Boxed warning: inadequate immune response to vaccines — discontinue 28d-3mo before vaccination, withhold 28d after. Dogs 12+ months.
common_side_effects: Vomiting/nausea (15.5–22.1%), Diarrhea (12.6–19.9%), Lethargy (12.1–12.2%), Otitis externa (3.9–10.5%), Anorexia (4.9–9.4%), Dermal growths (1.5–8.8%), Elevated liver enzymes (3.9–5.5%), UTI (5.5–6.3%)

name: Apoquel
description: First-generation JAK1-preferring inhibitor for canine atopic dermatitis (1.8x JAK1/JAK2 selectivity). Typically twice daily for 14 days then once daily maintenance. Also available as chewable (NADA 141-555, 2023). Dogs 12+ months.
common_side_effects: Pyoderma (12.0%), Dermal lumps (12.0%), Otitis (9.9%), Vomiting (2.3–9.2%), Diarrhea (2.3–6.0%), Histiocytoma (3.9%), Cystitis (3.5%), Anorexia (1.4–3.2%), Lethargy (1.8–2.8%)

name: Cytopoint
description: Anti-IL-31 monoclonal antibody injection administered at vet clinic. Blocks itch signal only, not broader inflammation. USDA-licensed biologic.
common_side_effects: Adverse events at rates similar to placebo — no drug-attributable percentages. EMA: anaphylaxis/facial swelling (rare)

name: Atopica
description: Immunosuppressant (calcineurin inhibitor) for canine atopic dermatitis. Slower onset (4-6 weeks). Give 1hr before or 2hr after meals.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (30.9%), Diarrhea (20.0%), Otitis externa (6.8%), UTI (3.8%), Anorexia (3.0%), Lethargy (2.3%), Gingival hyperplasia (2.3%), Lymphadenopathy (2.3%)

name: Cortavance
description: Topical corticosteroid spray for inflammatory and pruritic skin conditions. Short-course (7 consecutive days). Minimal systemic absorption. For localized flares/hot spots. EMA-authorized (not FDA).
common_side_effects: One transient local reaction (1/54, 1.9%) — aggravated erythema, resolved by Day 7. No systemic adverse effects. HPA axis suppression at 3–5X dose (reversible)

name: Genesis Spray
description: Topical corticosteroid spray for pruritus associated with allergic dermatitis. FDA-approved tapering regimen: BID x7d, SID x7d, EOD x14d (28 days total).
common_side_effects: Polyuria (5.3%), Polydipsia (5.3%), Polyphagia (1.8%), Local reactions/sneezing/watery eyes (≤3.6% — pooled across both groups)

name: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl)
description: First-generation antihistamine. Most commonly used canine antihistamine. OTC but vet-directed dosing. Sedating.
common_side_effects: Sedation/drowsiness (common), Dry mouth (common), Urinary retention (uncommon), GI upset (uncommon)

name: Hydroxyzine (Atarax)
description: First-generation antihistamine. Most prescribed Rx antihistamine for canine atopic dermatitis. Sedating.
common_side_effects: Sedation/drowsiness (common), Fine tremors at high doses (uncommon), GI upset (uncommon)

name: Cetirizine (Zyrtec)
description: Second-generation antihistamine. Less sedating. Increasingly recommended by veterinary dermatologists. Known as Reactine in Canada.
common_side_effects: Sedation/drowsiness (uncommon — 2nd-gen, less sedating), Vomiting (rare), Hypersalivation (rare)

name: Temaril-P
description: Combination antihistamine + low-dose steroid for allergic dermatitis and pruritus. FDA-approved for dogs. Known as Vanectyl-P in Canada.
common_side_effects: Sedation/drowsiness (common), Polydipsia/polyuria (common with prolonged use), Increased appetite (expected — prednisolone component)

name: Ketoconazole
description: Oral antifungal for Malassezia yeast dermatitis (common secondary to atopic dermatitis). Requires liver monitoring. Give with food for absorption. More hepatotoxic than itraconazole.
common_side_effects: Lack of appetite, Vomiting, Diarrhea, Liver toxicity (requires monitoring — liver enzymes every 2-3 months), Lightened coat color with prolonged use

name: Itraconazole (Sporanox)
description: Preferred systemic antifungal for Malassezia yeast dermatitis and dermatophytosis. Better tolerated than ketoconazole (less hepatotoxic). Give with food.
common_side_effects: Lack of appetite, Vomiting, Weight loss, Liver toxicity (requires monitoring)

name: Simparica Trio
description: Flea, tick, heartworm, roundworm, and hookworm prevention.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (14.3%), Diarrhea (13.2%), Lethargy (8.5%), Anorexia (5.1%), Polyuria (3.7%), Polydipsia (2.2%), Hyperactivity (2.2%)

name: Simparica
description: Isoxazoline flea and tick prevention.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (0.95%), Diarrhea (0.63%), Lethargy (0.32%)

name: NexGard
description: Isoxazoline flea and tick prevention. Most prescribed flea/tick product in North America.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (4.1%), Dry/flaky skin (3.1%), Diarrhea (3.1%), Lethargy (1.7%), Anorexia (1.2%)

name: NexGard PLUS
description: Flea, tick, heartworm, roundworm, and hookworm prevention. FDA approved 2023.
common_side_effects: Diarrhea (6.7%), Vomiting (4.5%), Lethargy (2.2%), Itching (2.2%), Dermatitis (1.5%)

name: NexGard Spectra
description: Flea, tick, heartworm, roundworm, hookworm, and whipworm prevention.
common_side_effects: Vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, anorexia, pruritus (each uncommon, 0.1–1%)

name: Bravecto
description: Flea and tick prevention with 12-week dosing interval (8 weeks for lone star ticks). Also available as topical.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (7.1%), Decreased appetite (6.7%), Lethargy (5.4%), Diarrhea (4.9%), Polydipsia (1.8%), Flatulence (1.3%)

name: Credelio
description: Isoxazoline flea and tick prevention. Smallest tablet size of the isoxazoline class.
common_side_effects: Weight loss (1.5%), Elevated BUN (1.0%), Polyuria (1.0%), Diarrhea (1.0%)

name: Credelio Plus
description: Flea, tick, heartworm, and roundworm prevention. Also treats demodicosis.
common_side_effects: Diarrhea (8.8%), Vomiting (8.2%), Anorexia (5.0%), Lethargy (3.1%)

name: Credelio Quattro
description: Flea, tick, heartworm, roundworm, hookworm, and tapeworm prevention. The only isoxazoline combo that covers tapeworms. FDA approved October 2024.
common_side_effects: Diarrhea w/ or w/o blood (11.0%), Vomiting (9.4%), Lethargy (6.3%), Anorexia (5.8%), Dermatitis (5.2%), Weight loss (3.1%)

name: Revolution
description: Flea, heartworm, ear mite, and sarcoptic mange prevention. Also treats roundworms.
common_side_effects: Vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, salivation (each ≤0.5% of dogs and cats combined)

name: Advantage Multi
description: Flea, heartworm, roundworm, hookworm, whipworm, and lungworm prevention.
common_side_effects: Pruritus (14.8%), Residue at site (7.0%), Medicinal odor (3.9%), Hyperactivity (0.8%), Lethargy (0.8%), Inappetence (0.8%)

name: K9 Advantix II
description: Flea, tick, mosquito, biting fly, and lice treatment. Repels and kills on contact. TOXIC TO CATS.
common_side_effects: Skin irritation/redness at site, scratching, lethargy, vomiting (all uncommon — no clinical trial %)

name: Frontline Plus
description: Flea and tick treatment. Older product, now somewhat superseded by isoxazolines.
common_side_effects: Application-site irritation (uncommon). Very rarely: lethargy, vomiting, diarrhea

name: Seresto
description: Flea and tick prevention collar providing up to 8 months of protection. One of the most widely used flea/tick products by volume. Regulated by EPA (pesticide), not FDA.
common_side_effects: Application-site reactions: itching, redness, slight hair loss. Uncommon but more serious: neurologic symptoms

name: Heartgard Plus
description: Heartworm, roundworm, and hookworm prevention.
common_side_effects: Vomiting or diarrhea within 24hrs (1.1% of administered doses — not per dog)

name: Interceptor Plus
description: Heartworm, roundworm, hookworm, whipworm, and tapeworm prevention.
common_side_effects: Vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, ataxia, anorexia (reported — no specific field trial %)

name: ProHeart 6
description: Injectable heartworm preventative providing 6 months of protection. Also treats hookworms. Administered by veterinarian.
common_side_effects: Anaphylaxis (uncommon), vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy (post-market)

name: ProHeart 12
description: Injectable heartworm preventative providing 12 months of protection. Also treats hookworms. FDA approved 2019. Available in US, AU/NZ, select EU countries. Canada has ProHeart 6 only. Administered by veterinarian.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (25.3%), Lethargy (15.5%), Diarrhea (14.5%), Anorexia (13.8%), Seizures (3.4%), Hepatopathy (2.7%), Hypersalivation (2.4%), Anaphylactoid reactions (2.0%)

name: Bravecto Quantum
description: First annual flea and tick injectable for dogs. Single subcutaneous dose provides 12 months of protection. AU/NZ 2023, EU 2024, US FDA July 2025, Canada Sep 2025. Administered by veterinarian.
common_side_effects: Lethargy (4.9%), Decreased appetite (4.4%), Vomiting (4.0%), Diarrhea (2.7%), Elevated liver enzymes (2.7%), Pruritus (1.8%), Injection site lumps (1.3%), Seizures (0.9%)

name: Drontal Plus
description: Broad-spectrum dewormer for tapeworms (including Echinococcus), hookworms, roundworms, and whipworms. Treatment-focused, not a monthly preventative.
common_side_effects: No drug-related side effects in field study (n=103). Lab studies: vomiting and soft stool at 5X dose

name: Panacur
description: Broad-spectrum dewormer for roundworms, hookworms, whipworms, and Taenia tapeworms. Given over 3 consecutive days (label); 5 days off-label for Giardia.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (~1%, 3/240 dogs). Prolonged off-label use: pancytopenia/bone marrow hypoplasia (12 reports as of Oct 2023)

name: Metronidazole
description: Antibiotic and antiprotozoal for GI infections, Giardia, and inflammatory bowel disease. Also known as Flagyl. Very bitter taste; do not crush.
common_side_effects: Nausea/anorexia (common), Hypersalivation (common — bitter taste), Vomiting (common), Significant fecal microbiome dysbiosis lasting 4+ weeks. Neurotoxicity at high doses — ataxia, nystagmus, head tilt, seizures (uncommon, dose-dependent >60 mg/kg/day)

name: Cerenia
description: Anti-nausea and anti-vomiting medication. The only FDA-approved veterinary antiemetic for dogs. Also available as injection at vet clinic.
common_side_effects: Hypersalivation (common), Vomiting unrelated to motion (common), Lethargy (uncommon), Injection-site pain on subcutaneous administration (common)

name: Metoclopramide
description: Prokinetic and antiemetic that stimulates gastric motility and prevents esophageal reflux. Also known as Reglan.
common_side_effects: Restlessness/hyperactivity (uncommon), Extrapyramidal signs — muscle spasms, twitching (uncommon, dose-dependent), Drowsiness (uncommon), Constipation (uncommon)

name: Famotidine
description: H2-receptor antagonist acid reducer for gastritis and acid reflux. Available over-the-counter (Pepcid). Best given on empty stomach before meals.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (rare), Diarrhea (rare), Decreased appetite (rare)

name: Omeprazole
description: Proton pump inhibitor for ulcers and severe acid reflux. Available over-the-counter (Prilosec). Do not crush capsules.
common_side_effects: GI adverse effects (2 dogs with GI signs: one with diarrhea+hematochezia, one with nausea→vomiting→diarrhea), Hypergastrinemia — elevated gastrin in 100% of dogs by day 30 (clinically silent)
side_effects_sources: [Gil-Vicente et al. 2024 Animals](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38672316/) (60-day trial; methods: n=8 omeprazole group, AE section: 2/11)

name: Sucralfate
description: Coats and protects stomach and intestinal lining. Give on empty stomach, separated from other meds by 2 hours. Also known as Sulcrate/Carafate. Also available as liquid suspension.
common_side_effects: Constipation (~2% — extrapolated from human data). Essentially no systemic side effects — not absorbed. Primary concern is drug interactions (binds other meds — separate by 2hrs)

name: Misoprostol
description: Prostaglandin E1 analog gastroprotectant used to prevent NSAID-induced gastric ulceration. Commonly co-prescribed with long-term NSAID therapy. Also known as Cytotec.
common_side_effects: Diarrhea (common, dose-dependent — most frequent adverse effect), Abdominal cramping (common), Vomiting (common), Abortion in pregnant animals (contraindicated)

name: Budesonide
description: Locally-acting corticosteroid for inflammatory bowel disease. High first-pass hepatic metabolism. Do not open or crush capsules.
common_side_effects: Polydipsia (common), Excessive panting (common), Polyuria (common), Increased appetite (common), Lethargy (uncommon), Elevated ALP/ALT (common)

name: Azathioprine
description: Immunosuppressive for inflammatory bowel disease when steroids alone are insufficient. Standard second-line IBD therapy. Also known as Imuran. Requires regular blood monitoring.
common_side_effects: Hepatotoxicity/elevated ALT (15%), Bone marrow suppression — leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia (8.3%), Anorexia (common), Vomiting (common), Diarrhea (common), Acute pancreatitis (uncommon)
side_effects_sources: [Wallisch & Trepanier 2015 JVIM](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25641386/) (52 dogs enrolled; different subsets had different follow-up); [Eberhardy et al. 2022 Vet Derm](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36000613/) (n=41, alternate-day: hepatotox 4.9%)

name: Tylosin
description: Macrolide antibiotic for chronic diarrhea and antibiotic-responsive enteropathy. Extremely bitter; often placed in gelatin capsules for dosing. Also known as Tylan. Labeled for livestock; canine use is off-label.
common_side_effects: Bitter taste (primary practical issue — requires capsule), Mild GI upset (rare), Fecal microbiome shifts (documented). Diarrhea commonly recurs after discontinuation
side_effects_sources: [Kilpinen et al. 2011 Acta Vet Scand](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21489311/) (n=27); [Manchester et al. 2019 JVIM](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31674054/) (microbiome, n=16)

name: Ondansetron
description: 5-HT3 serotonin receptor antagonist antiemetic for severe nausea and vomiting. Also known as Zofran. Use caution in MDR1-positive breeds (collies, sheepdogs).
common_side_effects: Sedation/drowsiness (uncommon), Constipation (uncommon), Transient liver enzyme elevation (rare)
side_effects_sources: [VCA monograph](https://vcahospitals.com/know-your-pet/ondansetron); [Foth et al. 2021 BMC Vet Res](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34154584/) (n=16, no AEs reported)

name: Sulfasalazine
description: Anti-inflammatory for large bowel disease (colitis) and vasculitis. Also known as Salazopyrin. Give with food.
common_side_effects: GI upset — nausea, vomiting, diarrhea (common), Keratoconjunctivitis sicca/dry eye (common with prolonged use — requires Schirmer test monitoring), Cholestatic jaundice (rare)

name: Mesalamine
description: 5-ASA anti-inflammatory for colitis. Same active component as sulfasalazine but without the sulfonamide carrier. KCS risk still applies (5-ASA itself is causative) — requires Schirmer test monitoring. Also known as Asacol/Pentasa.
common_side_effects: GI upset (expected — same active moiety as sulfasalazine), KCS/dry eye with prolonged use (requires Schirmer test monitoring), Nephrotoxicity (rare)

name: Loperamide
description: Antidiarrheal that slows intestinal motility. Available over-the-counter (Imodium). CONTRAINDICATED in MDR1/ABCB1 breeds (collies, Aussies, shelties).
common_side_effects: Constipation (common — expected effect), Bloat/GI distension (uncommon), Sedation (uncommon in normal dogs; severe in MDR1-mutant breeds — contraindicated). CNS toxicity in MDR1 breeds — ataxia, mydriasis, coma

name: Pancrelipase
description: Pancreatic enzyme replacement (lipase, amylase, protease) for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (EPI). Mixed directly into food with every meal. Also known as Viokase/Pancrezyme.
common_side_effects: Oral bleeding (reported — resolves with dose reduction), Diarrhea (uncommon — dose-related), Vomiting (uncommon), Perioral irritation from enzyme contact (uncommon)
side_effects_sources: [Rutz et al. 2002 JAVMA](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12494968/) (3 EPI dogs with oral bleeding)

name: Clavamox
description: One of the most commonly prescribed veterinary antibiotics. Used for GI bacterial infections and secondary skin infections from allergic dermatitis. Also known as Augmentin (human equivalent). Give with food.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (common), Diarrhea (common), Anorexia (uncommon)

name: Cephalexin
description: First-generation cephalosporin antibiotic. First-line treatment for pyoderma (bacterial skin infections secondary to allergies).
common_side_effects: Vomiting (common), Diarrhea (common), Anorexia (uncommon)

name: Cefpodoxime (Simplicef)
description: Third-generation cephalosporin antibiotic. Convenient once-daily alternative to cephalexin for canine pyoderma. Generics available.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (common), Diarrhea (common), Decreased appetite (uncommon)

name: Clindamycin (Antirobe)
description: Lincosamide antibiotic for deep pyoderma, dental infections, and bone infections. Commonly prescribed for allergy-related skin infections. Significant GI side effects (diarrhea).
common_side_effects: Vomiting (common), Diarrhea (common), Anorexia (uncommon)

name: Convenia
description: Long-acting injectable cephalosporin — single subcutaneous injection provides 14 days of antibiotic coverage. FDA-approved for skin infections; also used off-label for UTIs. Administered by veterinarian.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (common), Diarrhea (common), Lethargy (uncommon), Anorexia (uncommon). Single injection with 14-day duration — side effects cannot be stopped once administered

name: Enrofloxacin (Baytril)
description: Fluoroquinolone antibiotic for deep/resistant pyoderma, UTIs, and some GI infections. Reserved for infections unresponsive to first-line antibiotics.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (common), Diarrhea (common), Anorexia (uncommon), Retinal toxicity at high doses (rare — avoid in cats)

name: Metacam
description: Oxicam-class NSAID oral suspension for pain and inflammation from osteoarthritis and musculoskeletal disorders. Comes with calibrated dosing syringe.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (16.7–29.4%), Diarrhea/soft stool (6.3–13.8%), Inappetence (2.8–4.2%), GI ulceration (rare — at 3–5X dose), Elevated liver enzymes (rare)
side_effects_sources: [FDA NADA 141-213 FOI](https://animaldrugsatfda.fda.gov/adafda/app/search/public/document/downloadFoi/736) (Study #1 n=109 treated, Study #2 n=48 treated); [DailyMed label](https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=c27c35d7-07e1-47d4-97cd-ed1fb17750ce)

name: Rimadyl
description: Propionic acid-class NSAID for pain and inflammation from osteoarthritis and post-surgical recovery. Available as chewable tablets and caplets.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (3.1%), Diarrhea (3.1%), Inappetence (1.6%), Elevated ALP (7.8%), Elevated ALT (5.4%). Post-market: hepatic toxicity (rare — ~25% of hepatic AE reports involved Labradors), GI ulceration (rare), IMHA (rare)
side_effects_sources: [DailyMed label](https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=dbc05493-d1cc-4c00-be6b-d1e3352273fd) (OA n=129 vs n=132 placebo; postop n=148 vs n=149 placebo)

name: Previcox
description: Selective COX-2 inhibitor NSAID for osteoarthritis pain and postoperative pain from soft tissue and orthopedic surgery.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (3.9%), Decreased appetite (2.3%), Diarrhea (0.8%), Lethargy (0.8%). GI ulceration rare at supratherapeutic doses

name: Deramaxx
description: COX-2 selective NSAID for osteoarthritis, orthopedic surgery, and dental surgery pain. Flexible dosing — lower for chronic OA, higher for short-term post-surgical.
common_side_effects: Postop (3–4 mg/kg): Vomiting (10.5%), Diarrhea (5.7%), Hematochezia (3.8%), Hematuria (1.9%), Elevated ALT (2.9%). OA (1–2 mg/kg): Vomiting (2.9%), Diarrhea (2.9%)
side_effects_sources: [FDA NADA 141-203 FOI](https://animaldrugsatfda.fda.gov/adafda/app/search/public/document/downloadFoi/717) (postop n=105); [FDA NADA 141-203 OA supplement FOI](https://animaldrugsatfda.fda.gov/adafda/app/search/public/document/downloadFoi/718) (OA n=105+104)

name: Onsior
description: Highly selective COX-2 inhibitor NSAID that concentrates at inflammation sites. For OA pain (unlimited duration) and post-op soft tissue surgery pain (max 3 days).
common_side_effects: Diarrhea/soft stool (5.0%), Vomiting (5.0%), Decreased appetite (2.5%), Weight loss (0.8%). Post-market (foreign): elevated liver enzymes, hepatic necrosis, and death reported with long-term use

name: Galliprant
description: EP4 prostaglandin receptor antagonist for osteoarthritis pain and inflammation. Does not inhibit COX enzymes — distinct mechanism from traditional NSAIDs.
common_side_effects: Vomiting (17.0% vs 6.3% placebo), Diarrhea/soft stool (12.1% vs 9.0%), Anorexia (6.4% vs 4.9%), Lethargy (4.3% vs 1.4%), Buccal ulcer (0.7%), IMHA (0.7%)

name: Librela
description: Anti-NGF monoclonal antibody injection for osteoarthritis pain. Administered at vet clinic. FDA approved May 2023.
common_side_effects: UTI (11.1%), Bacterial skin infection (8.1%), Dermatitis (7.4%), Dermal mass (5.9%), Erythema (4.4%), Injection site pain (3.0%). Post-market: neurologic events (ataxia, seizures, paresis, paralysis), musculoskeletal adverse events, death/euthanasia (reported)
side_effects_sources: [FDA NADA 141-562 FOI](https://animaldrugsatfda.fda.gov/adafda/app/search/public/document/downloadFoi/13959) (n=135 treated vs n=137 control); [FDA Dear Vet letter Dec 2024](https://www.fda.gov/animal-veterinary/product-safety-information/dear-veterinarian-letter-notifying-veterinarians-about-adverse-events-reported-dogs-treated-librela)

name: Gabapentin
description: Anticonvulsant used off-label for chronic and neuropathic pain. Often used as adjunct alongside NSAIDs for multimodal pain management. Also commonly used for anxiety.
common_side_effects: Sedation (46% overall; more likely at doses >30 mg/kg), Agitation (24%), Ataxia (18%), Increased activity (14%), Aggression (6–8%), Increased appetite (6%), Diarrhea (4%)
side_effects_sources: [Kirby-Madden et al. 2024 Animals](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38791679/) (n=50); not FDA-approved for dogs

name: Tramadol
description: Synthetic opioid for moderate to moderately severe pain. Used as part of multimodal pain management. Controlled substance.
common_side_effects: Sedation (common), Nausea/vomiting (common), Constipation (common), Diarrhea (common), Anxiety/agitation (uncommon). Serotonin syndrome risk with SSRIs/MAOIs (rare)
side_effects_sources: [Donati et al. 2021 Vet Anaesth Analg](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33745825/) (systematic review, 26 RCTs); not FDA-approved for dogs

name: Prednisone
description: Broad corticosteroid and immunosuppressant. Used across many conditions: allergies, IBD, immune-mediated disease. Taper required when discontinuing.
common_side_effects: Increased water consumption (common — 55% reported filling bowl 2x+ as often by day 5), Polyphagia (~47% by day 14), Panting (~50% by day 14), Urinary accidents (23% by day 5, 35% by day 14), Behavioral changes — anxiety, aggression, lethargy (26–36% by day 14), Muscle wasting (chronic use), Steroid hepatopathy/elevated ALP (chronic use), Skin thinning (chronic use), Iatrogenic Cushing's (chronic use)
side_effects_sources: [Gober & Hillier 2023 BMC Vet Res](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37488543/) (n=31 prospective owner survey); [Elkholly et al. 2020 Front Vet Sci](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32923470/) (VetCompass, n=3,000)

name: Prednisolone
description: Active metabolite of prednisone — identical effects. Preferred for dogs with hepatic insufficiency (prednisone requires liver conversion). Some vets prescribe this instead of prednisone.
common_side_effects: Same active metabolite as prednisone — identical side effect profile. Polydipsia/polyuria (common), Polyphagia (common), Panting (common), Urinary accidents (common), Behavioral changes (common), Muscle wasting (chronic use), Steroid hepatopathy/elevated ALP (chronic use)
side_effects_sources: [Gober & Hillier 2023 BMC Vet Res](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37488543/) (study used both prednisone and prednisolone)

name: Dexamethasone
description: ~5X more potent than prednisone (~25X hydrocortisone). Used for severe allergic reactions, IBD, and autoimmune skin conditions. Often sent home as oral tablets after initial injectable dose at clinic.
common_side_effects: ~5X more potent than prednisone — same side effect categories (PU/PD, polyphagia, panting, behavioral changes) at proportionally lower doses. Immunosuppression, GI ulceration risk, adrenal suppression
